element_introduction_order.mastery_context: player draws tiles and begins forming words on board
player learns premium squares (double/triple letter and word scores) through gameplay
player must understand dictionary constraints and challenge mechanics
player learns point values increase with board position and tile selection
player competes against computer or human opponents with turn-taking discipline

rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: turn-based puzzle with variable tension based on tile randomness and opponent skill; cumulative score pressure increases toward game end

difficulty_curve.early_game: learning board layout and premium squares; establishing vocabulary familiarity
difficulty_curve.mid_game: optimizing tile placement on premium squares; managing hand quality
difficulty_curve.late_game: strategic constraint management; board space scarcity forces creative word combinations
notable_spikes: when playing against computer opponents with high difficulty settings
endgame when board becomes crowded and valid word placement options diminish

notable_level_patterns.description: Scrabble video games present a single continuous session where players take turns placing tiles on a shared board; difficulty emerges from tile randomness and opponent strength rather than discrete level progression
Available board space and remaining tile pool create natural difficulty escalation without explicit level structure; endgame becomes more constrained as board fills

extraction_notes: Source material is primarily platform/release history data. Limited gameplay mechanics documentation available. T3 tier: qualitative inferences about game structure based on Scrabble board game design principles. NES-specific implementation details not documented in source.
